question: What drew you to Politics and International Relations rather than, say, History or Economics?
lookingFor: A specific, personal account of why this discipline — not a generic interest in world affairs.

bullets: Identify the precise moment or trigger — a book, event, or experience — that pointed toward politics specifically
Distinguish what IR/Politics offers that adjacent subjects don't
Connect it to something you want to understand or do
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: The 2022 energy crisis was the moment it crystallised for me. I was watching the news and realised that what looked like an economics story was really a story about leverage, alliances, and historical grievance — and that none of my History or Economics lessons gave me the vocabulary to analyse it properly. IR sits at that intersection: it takes ideas seriously but insists they happen in a world of power and interest. I want to understand how states actually behave, not just how they're supposed to behave, and that's what pulled me to this over the neighbouring disciplines.

commonMistakes: A vague 'I'm interested in current affairs' with nothing specific behind it
Describing an interest that sounds identical to History or Economics
Naming a trigger but failing to connect it to the discipline's specific methods or questions
followUps: Which event in the last two years has most changed how you think about international politics?

question: Tell us about a book, article, or podcast that genuinely changed how you think about politics.
lookingFor: Genuine intellectual engagement — the ability to say what the work argued, why it was persuasive or flawed, and how it shifted your thinking.

bullets: Name the work and summarise its central argument concisely
Explain specifically what you found compelling or what surprised you
Say how it changed or complicated something you previously thought
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: Reading Thucydides' account of the Melian Dialogue for the first time was properly unsettling. Athens tells Melos that the strong do what they can and the weak suffer what they must — and Thucydides doesn't editorially condemn it, he just shows it. It forced me to take realism seriously rather than dismiss it as cynical. Before that I'd assumed international relations was basically a coordination problem that better institutions could solve. Now I think the question of whether morality can constrain power is genuinely hard, and that changed how I read everything else I picked up on the subject.

commonMistakes: Summarising the book without any personal reaction or critique
Naming a very well-known text but showing no real engagement with its argument
Claiming it 'changed everything' without saying what you thought before
followUps: What would you push back on in that argument?

question: What's the difference between a realist and a liberal view of international relations? Which do you find more convincing?
lookingFor: A clear, accurate contrast between the two traditions plus a reasoned, qualified personal view — not a textbook recitation.

bullets: Define each tradition accurately: realism on power and anarchy, liberalism on institutions, interdependence, and norms
Note where they disagree — on whether cooperation is durable, and on the role of domestic politics
Give a view that acknowledges the other side's strongest point
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: Realists argue that states operate in an anarchic system with no enforcer above them, so survival and relative power are the dominant concerns — cooperation is possible but always fragile. Liberals counter that trade, institutions, and shared norms have made cooperation stickier than realists expect: democracies rarely fight each other, and organisations like the WTO have genuinely constrained state behaviour. I lean liberal as a description of how the world works in normal times, but I think realism captures something important during moments of acute security competition — the war in Ukraine is hard to explain through a purely institutional lens. So I'd say the two are better read as complements than rivals.

commonMistakes: Conflating 'liberalism' the IR theory with 'liberalism' the domestic political ideology
Giving a textbook definition of each with no personal view at the end
Dismissing one tradition entirely rather than acknowledging its insights
followUps: Where would you place constructivism in relation to those two?

question: Pick a current geopolitical issue and explain the competing interests at play.
lookingFor: A structured analytical account that identifies multiple actors' interests and doesn't flatten a complex situation into a moral binary.

bullets: Choose a specific, bounded issue rather than a vague theme
Name at least three distinct actors and what each wants
Resist casting it as a simple good-versus-evil story
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I'd take the South China Sea disputes. China wants to consolidate control over waters it sees as historically sovereign and strategically essential for maritime access. The Southeast Asian claimants — Vietnam, the Philippines, Malaysia — are defending overlapping EEZ rights and don't want a precedent of large-power fait accompli. The US frames its presence as freedom of navigation but also has alliance commitments and an interest in preventing Chinese regional hegemony. ASEAN is trying to manage the dispute collectively while avoiding pushing China into a corner. What makes it genuinely hard is that all sides have at least a defensible legal or historical claim, so it's less a case of one aggressor than a collision of competing, incompatible legitimate interests.

commonMistakes: Presenting only one side's perspective
Describing the situation without identifying the underlying interests driving each actor
Choosing an issue so vague ('the Middle East') that the answer becomes superficial
followUps: What do you think the most likely resolution looks like, and over what time frame?

question: Is state sovereignty still meaningful in a world of globalisation and international institutions?
lookingFor: Nuanced reasoning about a core IR concept — not a yes/no answer but a qualified argument that takes both sides seriously.

bullets: Acknowledge that formal sovereignty remains the organising principle of the international system
Show how globalisation and institutions genuinely erode or complicate it in practice
Land on a qualified position — sovereignty has changed in character, not disappeared
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: Sovereignty is still the bedrock norm — states still treat each other as formally equal and independent, and it still decides who has a seat at the UN. But it's become much thinner in practice. Signing the WTO or the European Convention on Human Rights means accepting external oversight of domestic decisions that would once have been purely internal. Globalised capital markets constrain fiscal policy in ways no treaty needs to. So I'd say sovereignty has shifted from an absolute shield to a negotiated constraint — states still invoke it constantly, which is evidence it matters, but they do so in a world where they've already pooled or ceded pieces of it. The concept is alive but it needs qualifying.

commonMistakes: Asserting sovereignty is either fully intact or entirely obsolete without nuance
Conflating legal sovereignty with empirical or operational sovereignty
Ignoring strong counter-examples on either side of the argument
followUps: Does your answer look different for small states versus great powers?

question: What do you think is the greatest threat to liberal democracy today?
lookingFor: A specific, argued diagnosis — not a vague list — with some awareness of competing explanations.

bullets: Name one clear threat and explain the mechanism by which it undermines democracy
Acknowledge at least one alternative diagnosis and why you rank yours higher
Show awareness that the threat isn't purely external
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: My answer is democratic backsliding from within — elected leaders using democratic procedures to erode democratic constraints. Hungary is the clearest example: Orbán won legitimately and then used parliamentary majorities to pack courts, control media, and change electoral rules. What makes this harder to counter than an old-fashioned coup is that it has democratic legitimacy at each step. Economic inequality feeds it by generating the resentment that authoritarian populists channel, and social media makes elite capture of the information environment easier. I find this a more urgent threat than external authoritarianism because it's self-inflicted and much harder to diagnose from inside the system.

commonMistakes: Listing five threats with no prioritisation or argument for any of them
Treating democracy as purely under external threat and ignoring internal erosion
A confident answer with no acknowledgment of counter-arguments
followUps: Is there a country you think is handling this threat particularly well, and why?

question: Why have you applied to study Politics and IR here specifically, and what do you hope to get from the degree?
lookingFor: Specific, researched reasons tied to this department — modules, faculty, teaching approach — linked to a genuine intellectual ambition.

bullets: Name something specific to this department — a module, a research cluster, a teaching format
Connect it to a concrete intellectual interest or gap you want to fill
Articulate what you want the degree to do for how you think, not just what job it leads to
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I was drawn to the way your course structures the first year — grounding students in political theory and IR theory before branching into area studies. I've read enough to know I have opinions, but I want the conceptual rigour to test whether those opinions hold. The regional politics options in years two and three, especially the modules on Asia-Pacific security, line up with the area I find most analytically interesting right now. I also want to genuinely improve as a writer and a close-reader of difficult texts, and a politics degree at this level is one of the few places that trains that systematically.

commonMistakes: Generic praise about the university's rankings or reputation with no specific department research
A vague 'I want to understand the world better' without engaging with the course structure
Focusing entirely on career destinations rather than intellectual development
followUps: Is there a module in our prospectus you'd most want to take in final year, and why?

question: How would you define 'soft power', and give an example of a state using it effectively.
lookingFor: An accurate definition tied to Nye's original framing, a concrete and well-chosen example, and some awareness of its limits.

bullets: Define soft power as the ability to attract and co-opt rather than coerce — getting others to want what you want
Give a specific example with a clear mechanism, not just a country name
Note at least one limit or critique of soft power as a concept
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: Joseph Nye coined the term to describe power that works through attraction rather than coercion — getting other actors to want the outcomes you want without forcing them. South Korea is a compelling recent example: through K-pop, Korean cinema, and streaming platforms, Korean culture has generated significant goodwill globally, which has in turn made it easier for the Korean government to build trade relationships and diplomatic partnerships in Southeast Asia. The mechanism isn't direct — it's that cultural affinity creates a warmer context for harder diplomatic asks. The limit I'd flag is that soft power is slow and can evaporate if your hard-power behaviour contradicts your cultural appeal, which is a real tension for any state.

commonMistakes: Confusing soft power with foreign aid or economic incentives, which are closer to hard economic power
Naming a country without explaining the mechanism by which influence is exercised
No engagement with any limitation or critique
followUps: Can soft power actually change another state's foreign policy, or is it more about atmospherics?

question: Should states ever intervene militarily in another country to prevent mass atrocities, even without UN authorisation?
lookingFor: Structured ethical and political reasoning — a genuine position held after weighing competing principles, not evasion.

bullets: Acknowledge the core tension: sovereignty and non-intervention versus the moral imperative to prevent mass killing
Engage with the Responsibility to Protect framework and why the UN veto complicates it
Take a position and defend it, while conceding the strongest objection
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: This is one of the genuine hard cases in international ethics. The principled argument for intervention is that sovereignty shouldn't function as a licence for mass murder — the Responsibility to Protect doctrine captures the intuition that sovereignty is conditional on protecting your population. The problem is that without UN authorisation, you've removed the only multilateral check on abuse: states will always claim atrocities are occurring to justify interventions serving their interests. Kosovo is the case I keep coming back to — NATO acted without UNSC authorisation and the humanitarian outcome was broadly positive, but it set a precedent Russia and others have cited to justify their own actions since. My tentative position is that intervention can be legitimate without authorisation, but only with a very high evidential bar, regional consensus, and genuine multilateral backing short of a Security Council veto — accepting that this will always be contested and imperfect.

commonMistakes: Giving a confident 'yes' or 'no' without engaging with the strongest objection
Treating R2P as a settled legal framework rather than a contested norm
No engagement with specific cases — the argument stays abstract and untestable
followUps: Who gets to decide whether the evidential bar has been met?

question: If you were designing a political system from scratch, what would be its most important feature and why?
lookingFor: Political philosophy applied to a design question — shows the student can reason from principles, not just describe existing systems.

bullets: Name one feature and argue for it from a principle, not just from preference
Anticipate an obvious objection and respond to it
Show awareness that there are real trade-offs involved
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I'd make independent judicial review — the ability of courts to strike down government action — the most fundamental feature. My reasoning is Rawlsian in spirit: whatever we disagree about, we should want a system that constrains those in power from entrenching themselves. Without an independent court able to enforce that constraint, every other right is contingent on the goodwill of whoever governs. The obvious objection is democratic: unelected judges overriding elected majorities is anti-democratic. I think that's right as a concern but wrong as a verdict, because democracy requires structural protections to remain a real democracy — and those protections can't be left to the discretion of democratic majorities, who have an obvious interest in removing them.

commonMistakes: Describing an existing country's system without deriving it from any principle
Listing multiple features without prioritising or arguing for any of them
No engagement with the trade-offs or objections your design creates
followUps: How would you protect judicial independence from political capture?

question: Is the United Nations still fit for purpose? What, if anything, would you reform?
lookingFor: A balanced assessment that identifies structural weaknesses while not dismissing the institution's genuine achievements — and a reform idea that shows understanding of why reform is hard.

bullets: Name a specific structural weakness and explain why it matters
Acknowledge something the UN does that is genuinely valuable and would be missed
Propose a reform and show you understand the obstacle to achieving it
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: The Security Council veto is the most visible problem: a structure designed in 1945 for five great powers means that any permanent member can paralyse the body on issues where it has an interest, which is often precisely when action matters most. That said, the UN's specialised agencies — WHO, UNHCR, the WFP — do things that don't get headlines but would genuinely be missed: coordinating disease surveillance, managing refugee flows, food aid logistics. If I could reform one thing, I'd limit the veto to existential national security threats and require supermajority agreement to invoke it. The obstacle is obvious: you'd need the P5 to agree to limit their own power, which they won't do voluntarily. The realistic path is probably coalitions of member states using the General Assembly as a counter-pressure mechanism, which has precedents.

commonMistakes: Simply saying 'the UN is useless' without engaging with what it actually does
A reform so radical it ignores the political obstacles entirely
No awareness that member states, not the UN itself, are usually the obstacle to action
followUps: If you couldn't reform the UN, would you scrap it and start over?

question: What have you done outside the classroom that has deepened your interest in politics or international relations?
lookingFor: Genuine activity with genuine reflection — what you did, what you learned, and how it connects to wanting to study the subject.

bullets: Name a specific activity — not a vague 'following the news'
Say what it taught you that you couldn't have learned in a classroom
Connect it honestly to a question you now want to study
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I've been part of my school's Model United Nations for two years, most recently as delegate for Brazil at a conference on climate finance. What struck me wasn't the debating — it was how differently we each read the same proposal depending on which state we were assigned to. Playing a country whose priorities I found politically unsympathetic forced me to argue in good faith for positions I'd otherwise have dismissed. That experience made me take seriously the idea that interests, not just values, drive international negotiations, and it's part of what convinced me I want to study the theory behind that rather than just observe it.

commonMistakes: 'I watch the news every day' with no deeper engagement
Listing activities without reflecting on what they taught you
An activity that could belong to any applicant with no personal connection to the subject
followUps: What's something you believed about politics before that experience that you no longer believe?

question: What does a constructivist perspective add to our understanding of international relations that realism and liberalism miss?
lookingFor: An accurate account of constructivism's core insight — the role of ideas, norms, and identity — and a concrete example showing where it outperforms rationalist theories.

bullets: Explain constructivism's central claim: that interests and identities are socially constructed, not fixed material givens
Give a case where realism or liberalism struggles and constructivism explains it better
Note a limit or weakness of constructivism too
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: Constructivism argues that what states want — their interests — isn't just determined by material factors like military capability or economic size, but by the ideas, norms, and identities that shape how leaders and societies understand themselves and others. Realism and liberalism both treat interests as more or less fixed; constructivism asks where those interests come from. The abolition of the slave trade is a case realism handles badly: Britain was the dominant naval power and could have continued profiting. What changed was a normative shift driven by a social movement — the idea of the slave trade as morally intolerable became powerful enough to override material interests. Constructivism captures that. Its weakness is that it's better at explaining change after it happens than predicting it before, which limits its use as a policy guide.

commonMistakes: Confusing constructivism with a purely idealist position that ignores power
No concrete example — the answer stays entirely abstract
Explaining constructivism correctly but failing to show what the other theories miss
followUps: Can constructivism and realism be combined, or are they fundamentally incompatible?

question: Why is international cooperation on climate change so difficult to achieve, despite the shared interest in addressing it?
lookingFor: A structural analysis of the collective action problem — not a story about political bad faith — with awareness of what cooperation has and hasn't achieved.

bullets: Identify the collective action logic: costs are borne now and nationally, benefits are diffuse and future
Name the specific structural obstacles — free-rider incentives, divergent development levels, short-term electoral cycles
Acknowledge where cooperation has worked and why it's harder for climate than, say, ozone depletion
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: The core problem is that the incentive structure punishes action and rewards defection. Any country that limits its emissions bears a certain economic cost now, while the benefit — marginal reduction in global warming — is shared across the whole world regardless of who paid for it. That free-rider dynamic is hardest to solve when the number of actors is large and their interests are heterogeneous: a developed country that built its wealth on fossil fuels faces different pressures than a developing country that wants to do the same. The Montreal Protocol worked partly because there were few key actors and a clear substitute technology. Climate doesn't have either advantage. Paris is a genuine achievement in that it established a norm of nationally determined commitments, but without enforcement mechanisms it relies on reputational pressure, which is weaker than binding obligation.

commonMistakes: Blaming lack of cooperation entirely on political bad faith or individual leaders without structural analysis
Treating the Paris Agreement as either a full solution or a complete failure
No engagement with why some environmental cooperation has succeeded where climate hasn't
followUps: What mechanism would you add to the Paris framework to make it more effective?

question: What are the main trade-offs between first-past-the-post and proportional representation electoral systems?
lookingFor: A clear understanding of both systems and their political consequences — stability, representation, coalition dynamics — rather than advocacy for one.

bullets: Explain each system accurately: FPTP produces single-party governments, PR produces proportional legislatures
Name the key trade-off: FPTP delivers stability and accountability, PR delivers fairness and representation
Note the downstream consequences — coalition governments, entry of small parties, voter choice
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: FPTP tends to produce majority single-party governments even when no party wins a majority of votes, which makes government formation fast and electoral accountability clear — you know who to blame. The cost is that large chunks of votes are wasted and minority parties are systematically under-represented. Germany's MMP system and many Scandinavian PR systems produce legislatures much closer to the vote share — fairer, but typically requiring coalition negotiations that can take weeks and produce platforms no voter directly endorsed. There's no objectively correct answer; it depends on what you prioritise. Stable decisive government versus proportional representation. The UK's system has produced decisive majorities from 35% of the vote; Israel's pure PR has produced coalitions that collapse quickly. I find the comparison useful precisely because it shows that all electoral systems are a bundle of trade-offs, not a technical solution.

commonMistakes: Describing both systems without identifying the trade-offs clearly
Stating one system is simply better without acknowledging what it sacrifices
Confusing PR with coalition government — PR tends to produce coalitions, but they're distinct concepts
followUps: If you were advising a new democracy on which system to adopt, what would you ask before recommending?

question: Why do you think nationalism has become such a powerful political force in recent decades?
lookingFor: A structural, multi-causal analysis — not just 'social media' or 'bad politicians' — that takes the phenomenon seriously rather than dismissing it.

bullets: Identify at least two structural drivers — economic, cultural, or institutional
Show sympathy for why it appeals to people without endorsing its political excesses
Distinguish between civic nationalism and ethnic nationalism, or between nationalism as such and its illiberal forms
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: The resurgence is real and I think it has several reinforcing causes. Globalisation produced genuine economic winners and losers, and the losers — often in de-industrialised regions — had their concerns dismissed or attributed to their own failures rather than structural change. Alongside that, rapid demographic and cultural change felt threatening to communities with a strong sense of local identity that wasn't being acknowledged by mainstream politics. Nationalism gave both grievances a frame and a villain. The complicating point is that nationalism itself is neither inherently good nor bad: civic nationalism — loyalty to a shared political community — built welfare states. It's the ethnic, exclusionary version that's dangerous. Understanding why people find the national community meaningful seems more useful to me than treating it as simple irrationality.

commonMistakes: Explaining nationalism as simply the product of ignorance or manipulation
Only describing the phenomenon without explaining its causes
No distinction between nationalism as a political force and its most extreme or illiberal variants
followUps: Is there a tension between nationalism and liberal democracy, or can they coexist?

question: If there's no global government to enforce it, why do states comply with international law at all?
lookingFor: Engagement with the compliance puzzle — awareness of the academic debate between realist scepticism and liberal/institutionalist accounts of why law matters.

bullets: Acknowledge the realist puzzle: international law has no sheriff, so why follow it?
Name at least two reasons states comply — reputation, reciprocity, domestic politics, internalisation
Note that compliance is variable — not all international law is equally respected
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: The realist challenge is real: there's no world government to punish defection, so it seems rational to break rules when it's advantageous. But compliance is actually pretty high for large areas of international law — trade rules, diplomatic immunity, maritime law — and I think there are several explanations. Reputation matters: a state that breaks rules pays a credibility cost in future negotiations. Reciprocity matters: states comply partly because they want others to comply with the same rules toward them. And domestic politics matter — international legal norms are often internalised into domestic legal systems by courts and bureaucracies, so compliance becomes the path of least resistance even when political will is weak. Where law fails most visibly is usually in areas of acute security competition, which is consistent with the view that law works best when the costs of compliance are low and the benefits of the system as a whole are high.

commonMistakes: Asserting that states always follow international law — they clearly don't
Asserting that international law is meaningless because it can't be enforced — compliance rates tell a different story
No engagement with what drives variable compliance across different issue areas
followUps: Which area of international law do you think is most poorly enforced, and why?

question: Do you think the traditional left-right political spectrum is still a useful way to understand contemporary politics?
lookingFor: A thoughtful, qualified answer that engages with both the spectrum's continued relevance and its limits — evidence of real thinking about political categories.

bullets: Explain what the left-right distinction traditionally captures — economic redistribution, attitude to state intervention
Name at least one dimension of contemporary politics it handles poorly — cultural, authoritarian-libertarian, cosmopolitan-nationalist
Give a qualified verdict: useful but insufficient
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: The economic left-right axis still explains a lot: people's attitudes to redistribution, trade unions, and the role of the state cluster in predictable ways. But it handles the cultural and identity dimensions of contemporary politics badly. The biggest political divides in many countries right now are between cosmopolitans and nationalists, or between those who find rapid social change threatening and those who welcome it — and these don't map neatly onto left and right. You can be economically left-wing and culturally conservative, which the traditional spectrum struggles to accommodate. I'd say it's still useful as a shorthand for economic positions, but if you want to understand why people vote the way they do, you need at least a second axis. Inglehart's work on post-materialist values, or Mudde's cosmopolitan-populist distinction, fills in what the spectrum misses.

commonMistakes: Simply defending the spectrum or simply dismissing it without engagement with both sides
No concrete example of a political phenomenon the spectrum handles badly
Treating this as a question about your personal political preferences rather than a structural analytical question
followUps: What would a better framework look like?

question: Why do states go to war? Is war ever rational from a state's perspective?
lookingFor: Engagement with academic explanations of war — information failure, commitment problems, domestic politics — and a reasoned position on rationality.

bullets: Name at least two structural explanations for war beyond 'leaders are aggressive'
Engage with Fearon's rationalist explanations — information failure and commitment problems
Take a position on whether war can be rational while acknowledging the ex-post problem
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: War is puzzling from a rationalist perspective because it's costly for both sides — a negotiated settlement should theoretically leave both better off. Fearon's influential answer is that war happens when states can't credibly communicate their resolve or capabilities, so they fight to reveal the information that would have enabled a deal. Commitment problems are the other mechanism: if I can't trust you to honour a deal once the balance of power shifts, I might prefer to fight now rather than accept a settlement I know you'll renege on later. Domestic politics adds a third explanation — leaders sometimes go to war for internal reasons: rally effects, diversionary logic, pressure from constituencies. Is war ever rational? In the expected-value sense, yes — a state might calculate it will win decisively enough to outweigh costs. But ex post, most wars disappoint both sides relative to their initial expectations, which suggests the information failures are real and systematic. So I'd say war can be strategically rational ex ante but is usually ex-post regret.

commonMistakes: Treating war as simply the product of irrational or immoral leaders without structural analysis
Ignoring the bargaining model of war and its puzzles
Conflating war being rational with war being justified — these are completely different questions
followUps: Does nuclear deterrence change the rationality calculus for great-power war?

question: What is the relationship between a free press and a healthy democracy? Has social media changed it?
lookingFor: A clear account of why free press matters to democratic functioning, plus a nuanced assessment of social media's double-edged impact.

bullets: Explain the democratic function of a free press — accountability, agenda-setting, enabling informed voting
Identify social media's genuine democratic gains — access, citizen journalism, voice for the previously voiceless
Identify social media's democratic costs — algorithmic polarisation, disinformation economics, the collapse of verified information
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: A free press does three things democracy needs: it monitors the powerful and exposes wrongdoing, it sets the agenda of issues citizens consider important, and it provides a shared stock of verified information for citizens to vote on the basis of. Social media has expanded who can produce and distribute information, which has been genuinely democratising in contexts where the mainstream press was controlled or captured — Arab Spring-era journalism is the usual example. But the economics are perverse: engagement-maximising algorithms reward outrage and novelty, not accuracy. Disinformation is cheap to produce and expensive to rebut. And the shared informational commons that journalism once provided — even imperfect, even biased — has fragmented into parallel realities. So social media has simultaneously democratised voice and undermined the epistemic conditions democracy needs to function. I don't think that's resolvable without some form of platform accountability that doesn't also become censorship — which is the genuinely hard problem.

commonMistakes: Treating social media as simply harmful without acknowledging genuine democratic gains
No engagement with the mechanism — why does disinformation spread, not just that it does
Treating press freedom as self-evidently good without explaining what it does for democracy
followUps: How would you regulate disinformation without undermining free speech?

question: Do wealthy states have a moral obligation to poorer ones? How should that obligation, if it exists, be discharged?
lookingFor: Engagement with the normative question — cosmopolitan versus statist perspectives — plus a practical sense of what instruments exist.

bullets: Identify the core philosophical debate: cosmopolitan obligations to all humans versus special obligations to co-citizens
Take a position on whether an obligation exists and its grounds — historical, luck-based, or humanitarian
Discuss a specific mechanism and its limitations — aid, trade rules, debt relief, climate finance
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: The strongest argument for obligation is historical: the wealth gap between countries didn't emerge from neutral competition but from colonialism, resource extraction, and trade rules designed by the powerful. On that account, redistribution isn't charity — it's partial restitution. Even without that historical claim, a luck-of-birth argument — Rawls extended to the global level by Pogge — holds that being born in a poor country is morally arbitrary and a just global order would mitigate it. I find the historical argument most compelling. On how to discharge it: direct aid has a mixed record and can create dependency; reforming agricultural subsidies and trade rules would probably do more structural good; climate finance is increasingly urgent given that the poorest countries bear the highest climate costs from emissions they barely produced. None of these are politically easy because the obligation falls on voters who see distant strangers, not co-citizens.

commonMistakes: Treating this as purely a policy question without engaging with the normative debate
Dismissing the obligation question as unanswerable and jumping straight to mechanisms
No engagement with the tension between cosmopolitan and statist or communitarian views
followUps: Does the nature of the obligation change if climate change is the cause of the poverty?

question: Is conflict between the US and China inevitable, or is peaceful coexistence possible?
lookingFor: An informed, theoretically aware answer — not a prediction, but an analysis of the structural pressures and the factors that could mitigate them.

bullets: Introduce Thucydides Trap and why rising-power transitions create structural pressure
Identify specific areas of genuine conflict of interest versus areas of potential cooperation
Resist determinism — explain what would make conflict more or less likely
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: Graham Allison's Thucydides Trap argument — that rising powers and ruling ones have historically gone to war in most cases of power transition — is structurally compelling. The competition for regional dominance in Asia, control of semiconductor supply chains, and influence in global institutions is real and intensifying. But I'm cautious about determinism. The Soviet-US Cold War had comparable structural pressure and avoided direct military conflict for 45 years, partly because both sides maintained communication channels and drew lines. Economic interdependence between the US and China is an order of magnitude deeper than Soviet-US interdependence was, which raises the cost of conflict. Nuclear deterrence applies. Taiwan is the most plausible flashpoint, and risk is real — but the question is whether the leadership on both sides has enough control over escalation dynamics and enough to lose from conflict to pull back. Peaceful coexistence is possible; I'd say competitive coexistence is the most likely outcome.

commonMistakes: Treating conflict as inevitable and therefore requiring no further analysis
Treating peaceful coexistence as straightforward and ignoring the real structural pressures
Describing the US-China rivalry without the theoretical framing that IR theory provides
followUps: Which actor's decisions do you think matter most in determining whether conflict occurs?

question: Are human rights truly universal, or is the concept culturally specific?
lookingFor: Engagement with the universalism-relativism debate in political theory — a reasoned position, not a diplomatic non-answer.

bullets: Explain the universalist case: rights inhere in persons as persons, regardless of culture
Give the cultural relativist critique its due: who defines rights, and did non-Western societies consent to the norms?
Take a position — universalism, overlapping consensus, or qualified relativism — with reasons
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: The universalist case is compelling at the core: protections against torture, arbitrary killing, and enslavement track something about what it is to be a person rather than what culture you belong to. Most societies, pressed, have their own internal traditions against those things. The relativist critique has more force at the margins — rights to free assembly, particular models of gender equality — where the content does track specific political traditions and was often drafted without meaningful non-Western participation. I find Rawls's 'overlapping consensus' useful: rather than demanding everyone agree on philosophical foundations, we look for convergent endorsement of specific rights from within different traditions. That gets us most of the serious protections without requiring cultural homogeneity. Where I resist relativism entirely is when cultural practices are used to suppress internal dissent — the critique of 'Western values' is too often made by elites whose populations would vote differently if they could.

commonMistakes: Simply asserting rights are universal with no engagement with the relativist critique
Accepting full relativism without noting that it can be weaponised against the people it claims to protect
No engagement with specific examples of where the debate is sharp
followUps: Can a state violate human rights norms that it has never formally ratified?

question: Some argue that authoritarian governments can deliver development more effectively than democracies. How would you evaluate that claim?
lookingFor: A critical engagement with the 'authoritarian advantage' thesis — using evidence, acknowledging the selection bias problem, and reaching a reasoned conclusion.

bullets: Fairly state the authoritarian advantage argument — focus, speed, insulation from rent-seeking
Challenge it with the selection bias and survivorship bias problems — we cite Singapore but not Mobutu's Zaire
Bring in what democracy does for development that the thesis underweights — accountability, error-correction, human capital
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: The argument draws on cases like Singapore, South Korea under Park, and China — authoritarian states that achieved rapid industrialisation by insulating technocrats from distributional politics and mobilising resources at scale. The mechanism is real. But the thesis has a massive survivorship bias problem: we count the successes and forget the failures — Mobutu's Zaire, the Soviet Union's eventual stagnation, every petro-state that failed to diversify. Authoritarian development works when the leadership has genuine developmental intentions and state capacity; it's catastrophic when leadership is predatory, and you can't tell in advance which you've got. Democracies are messier but have error-correction mechanisms: bad policies can be reversed without revolution. Sen's work shows that no democracy with a free press has ever had a major famine — the informational and accountability mechanisms matter. I'd conclude: authoritarianism isn't an advantage, it's high variance. If you win the lottery on leadership it outperforms; most of the time you don't.

commonMistakes: Accepting the authoritarian advantage claim without addressing survivorship bias
Rejecting it entirely without engaging with the genuine cases where it seems to hold
No engagement with what democracy provides that authoritarianism can't — accountability, famine prevention, error-correction
followUps: What does your answer imply for how Western governments should engage with authoritarian developing states?

question: What makes a government legitimate? Can a government be legitimate if it wasn't chosen democratically?
lookingFor: A philosophically structured answer that distinguishes sources of legitimacy — consent, outcomes, procedure — and takes a reasoned position on the democratic question.

bullets: Distinguish sources of legitimacy: procedural (consent, elections), outcome-based (welfare, order), and normative (rights protection)
Show the tension: an elected government that oppresses minorities may be less legitimate than an unelected one that protects them
Take a position on whether democratic procedure is necessary or sufficient for legitimacy
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: Legitimacy is doing a lot of work in political theory and I think the confusion comes from conflating three different sources. Procedural legitimacy — the government came to power through agreed rules — is what democratic elections provide. Outcome legitimacy — the government delivers security, welfare, and rights — is what makes people comply even when they didn't choose the government. And there's a deeper normative legitimacy: whether the government respects the basic interests and dignity of those it governs. A government can be democratically elected and still fail normative legitimacy — Weimar Germany's government handed power to Hitler through procedurally valid mechanisms. Conversely, a technocratic body that was never elected might command wide acceptance if it delivers good outcomes and protects rights. My view is that democratic procedure provides the best available mechanism for achieving all three types of legitimacy, but it's neither necessary nor sufficient on its own — which is why constitutional limits on what majorities can do matter as much as elections.

commonMistakes: Treating democratic election as both necessary and sufficient for legitimacy without qualification
No engagement with the distinction between procedural, outcome, and normative legitimacy
A philosophical answer that never engages with a concrete case where the tension is sharp
followUps: Does legitimacy require the consent of the governed, or just their acceptance?
